title: Dog Hair Loss Around Eyes: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about 'goggle' hair loss in dogs, its common causes like mange or allergies, and why this low-urgency issue still needs a veterinary look.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hair loss around a dog's eyes, or "goggles," is typically caused by mites, ringworm, or allergies and is rarely an emergency unless the eye itself appears painful or cloudy.

What is "goggle" hair loss in dogs?

Hair loss around a dog's eyes, often called "goggles," refers to symmetrical thinning or bald patches that form a ring around the eye area. This isn't a single disease but a clinical symptom. It usually indicates that the hair follicles in that sensitive skin are being damaged or that the dog is rubbing the area due to irritation.

What are the common causes of hair loss around my dog's eyes?

  • Demodectic mange caused by microscopic mites
  • Fungal infections like ringworm
  • Environmental allergies
  • Autoimmune conditions such as uveodermatologic syndrome

When is hair loss around a dog's eyes considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is generally low and rarely life-threatening.
  • Schedule a vet visit if your dog is squinting or has a cloudy eye.
  • Seek medical attention if your dog is pawing at their face enough to cause bleeding.
  • An exam is necessary to rule out painful corneal ulcers or secondary infections.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eyes help with veterinary triage?

  • Clear, well-lit photos help professionals identify crusting, redness, or skin thickening.
  • Visual details help differentiate between parasitic issues like mites and inflammatory issues like allergies.
  • Photos allow the vet to prioritize your appointment and suggest the correct diagnostic tests.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Focal areas of alopecia (0.5-2 cm in diameter) may present with mild epidermal collarette formation at the margins of hair loss, although follicular pustules and erythema may be absent, particularly in short-haired breeds. Pattern baldness, characterized by asymptomatic hair thinning and eventual alopecia, has been reported affecting the pinna in Dachshunds, Chihuahuas, Italian Greyhounds, and Whippets, with onset typically around 1 year of age. Bacterial pyoderma is also a common cause of alopecia. Accurate diagnosis of hair loss requires a thorough history and physical examination, including consideration of breed predispositions for congenital or hereditary alopecias and assessment of the duration and progression of the hair loss.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 864)

faqs.question: Is goggle hair loss contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Ringworm is highly contagious to other pets and humans, whereas Demodectic mange and allergies are not.

faqs.question: Will the hair around my dog's eyes grow back?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases the hair will grow back once the underlying cause—such as a fungal infection or mites—is successfully treated.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on the hairless area?
faqs.answer: No. Never apply human medications or topical creams near your dog's eyes without a vet's approval, as many can be toxic or cause severe irritation to the eye itself.
